topic: Someone discovers their neighbor is secretly wealthy
raw_output:
[story 1]

The incessant drone of Mr. Henderson’s imported golf cart, belching faint, expensive-smelling exhaust, had been a soundtrack to my mornings for six months. I watched from behind my dusty window, my jaw tight enough to crack enamel. Every Tuesday and Thursday, he’d pull up, a gleaming silver behemoth, and emerge looking like he’d stepped off a magazine cover, even though he was just heading to the local park. I swear, the sheer volume of his automotive presence was an affront to the quiet, unassuming street we shared. I slammed my coffee mug down on the counter, the ceramic rattling sharply. "Honestly," I muttered to my reflection in the dark glass, "the sheer audacity of it all." He didn't even glance my way, just adjusted his silk tie with an air of supreme self-importance, as if I were merely a piece of unremarkable wallpaper.

[story 2]

I spent a good twenty minutes this afternoon attempting to assemble a flat-pack bookshelf, a task that, in itself, was a trial of patience. Then, the doorbell rang. It wasn't a delivery; it was Mr. Henderson, holding a pristine, leather-bound portfolio. He leaned against my doorframe, his smile wide and utterly unearned, the kind that usually precedes some grand announcement or, in my case, an intrusion. "Neighbor," he began, his voice smooth as aged whiskey, "I wanted to personally introduce myself. I hope I haven't been too intrusive." I felt a familiar tightening in my chest, a distinct urge to physically retreat. I took a slow, deliberate breath, forcing my expression into a mask of polite neutrality. "Mr. Henderson," I managed, my tone clipped, "I appreciate the outreach, but I’m quite busy right now."

[story 3]

It started subtly. The way his hedges seemed impossibly manicured, the sheer, silent opulence radiating from his property line. Then came the notices—glossy flyers tucked neatly into my mailbox, advertising some exclusive, off-market development. I found myself staring at the wrought-iron gate, my knuckles white where I gripped the railing. Why was he behaving this way? He never offered a greeting, never engaged in casual conversation. It was always these silent displays of effortless excess. I walked across the lawn, deliberately avoiding eye contact with the imposing stone facade of his home, feeling a deep, simmering resentment bubble up. "Just keep your ridiculous fortune to yourself," I thought, shaking my head slowly, the movement tight with suppressed frustration.
